hop2_docs: No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School | No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.  It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.

Praise Band: The Movie | Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.  It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.
Ransom Riggs | Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".

Dave Moody | David B. Moody is an artist, producer, songwriter and filmmaker from North Carolina.  His instrumental proficiency has earned him two Grammy Award Nominations and three International CMA Awards as a member of The Moody Brothers.  After becoming regular performers on the Grand Ole Opry and touring extensively throughout Europe in the 80s, the trio of Carolina brothers signed a contract with the Walt Disney Company in 1992 to open and perform their own nightly concerts at Disneyland Paris' Disney Village, where they performed for over 50 million guests during their time in France.

Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children | Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children is a contemporary fantasy debut novel by American author Ransom Riggs.  The story is told through a combination of narrative and vernacular photographs from the personal archives of collectors listed by the author.
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children (film) | Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children (film)

Hollow City (novel) | Hollow City is a 2014 dark fantasy novel and a sequel to "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children" written by Ransom Riggs.  It was released on January 14, 2014 by Quirk Books.  The novel is set right after the first, and sees Jacob and his friends fleeing from Miss Peregrine's to the "peculiar capital of the world", London.
summary_2:
Answer: Dave Moody fills a larger variety of entertainment roles than Ransom Riggs.

Supporting facts from the passages:
- Dave Moody (David B. Moody; member of The Moody Brothers):
  - Roles listed: artist, producer, songwriter, instrumentalist/musician, and filmmaker.
  - Honors/identifiers: two Grammy Award nominations and three International CMA Awards as a member of The Moody Brothers; the trio signed with Walt Disney Company in 1992 and performed at Disneyland Paris.
  - Film credits (with years and roles):
    - Praise Band: The Movie (2008) — produced and directed by Dave Moody.
    - No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School (2010) — directed by Dave Moody; his company also provided the musical score for the film.
  - These credits show music-related roles (performer, songwriter, composer/score provider) plus multiple filmmaking roles (director, producer).

- Ransom Riggs:
  - Identified roles: American writer and filmmaker.
  - Born February 3, 1979.
  - Best known for the novel Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children (the novel has a film adaptation noted in the passages), but the passages do not list separate producer/director/composer credits or additional entertainment roles beyond "writer and filmmaker."

Conclusion: The passages attribute a broader and more varied set of entertainment-industry roles to Dave Moody (musician/songwriter/producer/film director/film producer/score provider) than to Ransom Riggs (writer and filmmaker), so Dave Moody is able to fill a larger variety of roles.

hop2_docs: Georgia O'Keeffe | Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist.  She was best known for her paintings of enlarged flowers, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes.  O'Keeffe has been recognized as the "Mother of American modernism".

Barbara Buhler Lynes | Barbara Buhler Lynes is an art historian, curator, professor, and preeminent scholar on the art and life of Georgia O'Keeffe.  She is currently the Sunny Kaufman Senior Curator at the NSU Museum of Art in Fort Lauderdale, Florida.  From 1999-2012, she served as the founding curator of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, New Mexico, where she curated or oversaw more than thirty exhibitions of works by O’Keeffe and her contemporaries.  Lynes was also the Founding Emily Fisher Landau Director of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center from 2001-2012.  Prior to her work at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, Lynes served as an independent consultant to the National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. from 1992-1999 and has taught art history at Vanderbilt University, Dartmouth College, Montgomery College, and the Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA).

From 1918 to 1934, Georgia O'Keeffe spent part of the year at Alfred Stieglitz's family estate in Lake George.  The depicted shanty was O'Keeffe's studio, which was painted in subdued tones in response to criticism from Stieglitz' circle—Arthur Dove, John Marin, Charles Demuth, Marsden Hartley, and Paul Strand.  O'Keeffe said of the painting: "The clean, clear colors were in my head, but one day as I looked at the brown burned wood of the Shanty I thought, "I can paint one of those dismal-colored paintings like the men.  I think just for fun I will try—all low-toned and dreary with the tree beside the door."  "My Shanty" was the first painting by O'Keeffe purchased by the Duncan Phillips.

New York skyscrapers (O'Keeffe) | Georgia O'Keeffe created a series of paintings of New York skyscrapers between 1925 and 1929.  They were made after O'Keeffe moved with her new husband into an apartment on the 30th floor of the Shelton Hotel, which gave her expansive views of all but the west side of the city.  She expressed her appreciation of the city's early skyscrapers that were built by the end of the 1920s.  One of her most notable works, which demonstrates her skill at depicting the buildings in the Precisionist style, is the "Radiator Building—Night, New York", of the American Radiator Building.

Shown on Lifetime Television, it starred Joan Allen and Jeremy Irons in lead roles.

It opened on July 17, 1997, eleven years after the artist's death, and is located at 217 Johnson Street in Santa Fe, New Mexico, United States.

This series of works was completed following three summers of instruction at the University of Virginia on Arthur Wesley Dow's design philosophies, which were highly influential in her development as an abstract artist. - Identity and dates: Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986). An American modernist painter widely called the "Mother of American modernism."

- Primary subjects and style:
  - Best known for enlarged close-up flower paintings (painted from the mid-1920s through the 1950s), New York skyscraper series (1925–1929), and New Mexico landscapes.

question: Who is  Georgia O'Keeffe?
baseline_hop2_query: Georgia O'Keeffe Alfred Stieglitz relationship biography major works "Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1" "Black Iris" "Radiator Building—Night, New York" "Sky Above Clouds" catalogue raisonné exhibitions collections critical reception
hop2_query: Georgia O'Keeffe Alfred Stieglitz relationship biography major works "Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1" "Black Iris" "Radiator Building—Night, New York" "Sky Above Clouds" catalogue raisonné exhibitions collections critical reception
hop2_docs: Black Iris (painting) | Black Iris, sometimes called Black Iris III, is a 1926 oil painting of by Georgia O'Keeffe.  Art historian Linda Nochlin interpreted Black Iris as a morphological metaphor for female genitalia.  O'Keeffe rejected such interpretations in a 1939 text accompanying an exhibition of her work by writing: "Well—I made you take time to look at what I saw and when you took time to really notice my flower you hung all your own associations with flowers on my flower and you write about my flower as if I think and see what you think and see of the flower—and I don't."  She attempted to do away with sexualized readings of her work by adding a lot of detail.

Flower paintings of Georgia O'Keeffe | Georgia O'Keeffe is best known for her close-up,or large-scale flower paintings, which she painted from the mid-1920s through the 1950s.  She made about 200 paintings of flowers of the more than 2,000 paintings that she made over her career.  One of her paintings, "Jimson Weed", sold for $44.4 million, making it the most expensive painting sold of a female artist's work .

New York skyscrapers (O'Keeffe) | Georgia O'Keeffe created a series of paintings of New York skyscrapers between 1925 and 1929.  They were made after O'Keeffe moved with her new husband into an apartment on the 30th floor of the Shelton Hotel, which gave her expansive views of all but the west side of the city.  She expressed her appreciation of the city's early skyscrapers that were built by the end of the 1920s.  One of her most notable works, which demonstrates her skill at depicting the buildings in the Precisionist style, is the "Radiator Building—Night, New York", of the American Radiator Building.

Jimson Weed (painting) | Jimson Weed is an oil on linen painting by American artist Georgia O'Keeffe from 1936, located in the Indianapolis Museum of Art, which is in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States.  It depicts four large blossoms of jimson weed, or datura.

Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 | Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 represents Georgia O'Keeffe's first major exploration of abstract art and attainment of a freedom to explore her artistic talents based upon what she felt and envisioned.  O'Keeffe developed radical charcoal drawings, with just a few lines, that led to greater development of total abstraction.  This series of works was completed following three summers of instruction at the University of Virginia on Arthur Wesley Dow's design philosophies, which were highly influential in her development as an abstract artist.  Early the following year, photographer and art dealer, Alfred Stieglitz exhibited some of the drawings at his "291" art gallery.

Alfred Stieglitz | Alfred Stieglitz (January 1, 1864 – July 13, 1946) was an American photographer and modern art promoter who was instrumental over his fifty-year career in making photography an accepted art form.  In addition to his photography, Stieglitz was known for the New York art galleries that he ran in the early part of the 20th century, where he introduced many avant-garde European artists to the U.S.  He was married to painter Georgia O'Keeffe.

My Shanty, Lake George | My Shanty, Lake George is a 1922 painting by Georgia O'Keeffe.  From 1918 to 1934, Georgia O'Keeffe spent part of the year at Alfred Stieglitz's family estate in Lake George.  The depicted shanty was O'Keeffe's studio, which was painted in subdued tones in response to criticism from Stieglitz' circle—Arthur Dove, John Marin, Charles Demuth, Marsden Hartley, and Paul Strand.  O'Keeffe said of the painting: "The clean, clear colors were in my head, but one day as I looked at the brown burned wood of the Shanty I thought, "I can paint one of those dismal-colored paintings like the men.  I think just for fun I will try—all low-toned and dreary with the tree beside the door."  "My Shanty" was the first painting by O'Keeffe purchased by the Duncan Phillips.
summary_2:
- Identity and dates: Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986). An American modernist painter widely called the "Mother of American modernism."

- Primary subjects and style:
  - Best known for enlarged close-up flower paintings (painted from the mid-1920s through the 1950s), New York skyscraper series (1925–1929), and New Mexico landscapes.
  - Employed modernist and Precisionist approaches; also produced major abstract charcoal drawings (notably 1915).

- Notable works and facts:
  - Black Iris (Black Iris III), 1926 oil painting; O'Keeffe rejected sexualized interpretations of her flower paintings.
  - Jimson Weed (1936), oil on linen depicting jimson weed blossoms; associated with a sale of $44.4 million (noted as the most expensive painting sold of a female artist's work) and an example held at the Indianapolis Museum of Art.
  - Radiator Building—Night, New York (American Radiator Building) — a notable skyscraper painting demonstrating her Precisionist skill.
  - My Shanty, Lake George (1922) — painted while she spent part of each year at Alfred Stieglitz's Lake George estate; was the first O'Keeffe painting purchased by collector Duncan Phillips.

- Output and volume: Made more than 2,000 paintings over her career; about 200 were flower paintings.

- Education and influences:
  - Influenced by Arthur Wesley Dow's design principles; produced watercolors at the University of Virginia (1912–1914) and radical charcoal drawings in 1915 that marked a move toward abstraction.
  - Alfred Stieglitz (January 1, 1864 – July 13, 1946) — American photographer and modern art promoter who exhibited some of her early drawings at his "291" gallery and was her husband and major promoter.

- Residences, studios, and institutions:
  - Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio, 12 Palvadera Drive, Abiquiú, New Mexico — her principal residence and studio from 1945–1984; designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998; now a historic house museum managed by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum (guided tours available).
  - Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, 217 Johnson Street, Santa Fe, New Mexico — museum dedicated to her legacy; opened July 17, 1997.

- Associates and scholarship:
  - Maria Chabot (1913–2001): friend, advocate for Native American arts, contractor for O'Keeffe's Abiquiú house, and photographer; their correspondence was published.
  - Barbara Buhler Lynes: leading O'Keeffe scholar and founding curator/director at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center.

- Key contextual points:
  - Early work pre-1912 shows pre-Dow influence; major creative periods include early abstracts/charcoals (1915), NYC skyscraper series (1925–1929), and the long New Mexico period centered on Abiquiú (1945–1984).
  - O'Keeffe actively resisted reductionist/sexualized readings of her flower paintings, arguing viewers project their associations onto her subjects.
hop1_titles: O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914
hop2_titles: Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915

summary_2:
- Identity / Basic fact: Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) — American artist, widely regarded as a leading modernist painter and often called the "Mother of American modernism."

- Primary subjects & style: Best known for enlarged flower paintings, depictions of New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes; developed significant abstract and modernist work.

- Early training & dated activity:
  - Influenced by Arthur Wesley Dow's principles; studied those methods and created watercolors at the University of Virginia dated 1912–1914.
  - Produced important charcoal drawings in 1915 that marked a major exploration of abstraction; some of these drawings were exhibited by Alfred Stieglitz at his "291" gallery.
  - Early works exist from before 1912 (pre-Dow influence).

- Notable works & dates (from passages):
  - "My Shanty, Lake George" (1922) — painted during years she spent part of each year at Lake George; this was the first O'Keeffe painting purchased by Duncan Phillips.
  - Charcoal drawings (1915) — pivotal in her move toward abstraction.

- Residences, sites & institutional legacy (addresses and dates):
  - Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio, 12 Palvadera Drive, Abiquiú, New Mexico — O'Keeffe's principal residence and studio from 1945–1984; designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998; now a historic house museum managed by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum (guided tours available).
  - Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, 217 Johnson Street, Santa Fe, New Mexico — museum dedicated to her legacy, opened July 17, 1997.

- Associates & roles:
  - Alfred Stieglitz (January 1, 1864 – July 13, 1946) — American photographer and modern art promoter who was married to Georgia O'Keeffe and who exhibited her work; influential in promoting modern art in the U.S.
  - Maria Chabot — friend, advocate for Native American arts, contractor/photographer for O'Keeffe (correspondence published for years 1941–1949) (mentioned in context).
  - Barbara Buhler Lynes — leading O'Keeffe scholar and founding curator/director at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center (mentioned in context).

- Timeline highlights (concise):
  - Pre-1912: early works before Dow influence.
  - 1912–1914: watercolors at University of Virginia.
  - 1915: charcoal drawings and early abstraction; exhibited by Stieglitz.
  - 1918–1934: spent part of each year at Lake George (site of "My Shanty").
  - 1922: "My Shanty, Lake George" painted.
  - 1945–1984: principal residence/studio at Abiquiú, New Mexico.
  - 1986: O'Keeffe's death.
  - 1997: Georgia O'Keeffe Museum opened (July 17, 1997).
  - 1998: Abiquiú home/studio designated a National Historic Landmark.

- Cultural representations:
  - "Georgia O'Keeffe" (2009) — American television biographical film about O'Keeffe and her husband Alfred Stieglitz; directed by Bob Balaban; starred Joan Allen (O'Keeffe) and Jeremy Irons (Stieglitz); shown on Lifetime.

- Missing but relevant items noted for further follow-up (explicitly flagged in context):
  - Comprehensive list of major painting titles and their dates (beyond those cited).
  - Broader biographical details (early life, education beyond listed training, awards).
  - Full catalogue raisonné and detailed exhibition/critical reception history.

In short: Georgia O'Keeffe (1887–1986) was a central American modernist painter known for enlarged flowers, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes; trained under Arthur Wesley Dow principles, produced key works and abstractions in the 1910s–1920s, was married to and promoted by Alfred Stieglitz, lived and worked at 12 Palvadera Drive in Abiquiú (1945–1984), and is commemorated by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum (217 Johnson Street, Santa Fe; opened 1997).

After becoming regular performers on the Grand Ole Opry and touring extensively throughout Europe in the 80s, the trio of Carolina brothers signed a contract with the Walt Disney Company in 1992 to open and perform their own nightly concerts at Disneyland Paris' Disney Village, where they performed for over 50 million guests during their time in France.

It has about 150,000 inhabitants, while its metropolitan city (including Cagliari and 16 other nearby municipalities) has more than 431,000 inhabitants. hop2_docs: Barbara Buhler Lynes | Barbara Buhler Lynes is an art historian, curator, professor, and preeminent scholar on the art and life of Georgia O'Keeffe.  She is currently the Sunny Kaufman Senior Curator at the NSU Museum of Art in Fort Lauderdale, Florida.  From 1999-2012, she served as the founding curator of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, New Mexico, where she curated or oversaw more than thirty exhibitions of works by O’Keeffe and her contemporaries.  Lynes was also the Founding Emily Fisher Landau Director of the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center from 2001-2012.  Prior to her work at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, Lynes served as an independent consultant to the National Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. from 1992-1999 and has taught art history at Vanderbilt University, Dartmouth College, Montgomery College, and the Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA).

Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio | The Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio is a historic house museum at 12 Palvadera Drive in Abiquiú, New Mexico.  It was from 1945 until 1984 the principal residence and studio of artist Georgia O'Keeffe (1887-1986).  It is now owned and managed by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum in Santa Fe, through which guided tours can be arranged.  It was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998, as one of the most important artistic sites in the southwestern United States.

Jimson Weed (painting) | Jimson Weed is an oil on linen painting by American artist Georgia O'Keeffe from 1936, located in the Indianapolis Museum of Art, which is in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States.  It depicts four large blossoms of jimson weed, or datura.

Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 | Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 represents Georgia O'Keeffe's first major exploration of abstract art and attainment of a freedom to explore her artistic talents based upon what she felt and envisioned.  O'Keeffe developed radical charcoal drawings, with just a few lines, that led to greater development of total abstraction.  This series of works was completed following three summers of instruction at the University of Virginia on Arthur Wesley Dow's design philosophies, which were highly influential in her development as an abstract artist.  Early the following year, photographer and art dealer, Alfred Stieglitz exhibited some of the drawings at his "291" art gallery.

Flower paintings of Georgia O'Keeffe | Georgia O'Keeffe is best known for her close-up,or large-scale flower paintings, which she painted from the mid-1920s through the 1950s.  She made about 200 paintings of flowers of the more than 2,000 paintings that she made over her career.  One of her paintings, "Jimson Weed", sold for $44.4 million, making it the most expensive painting sold of a female artist's work .

O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914 | O’Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912-1914 is an exhibition of watercolors that Georgia O'Keeffe created over three summers in the early 20th century at the University of Virginia.  Shown at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, the exhibit opened November 4, 2016 and runs through September 10, 2017.

Georgia O'Keeffe | Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist.  She was best known for her paintings of enlarged flowers, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes.  O'Keeffe has been recognized as the "Mother of American modernism".
summary_2:
- Identity and dates: Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986) was an American artist and a leading modernist painter, widely recognized as the "Mother of American modernism."

- Primary subjects and style: Best known for enlarged flower paintings, depictions of New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes. She produced roughly 2,000 paintings in her career, about 200 of which are flower paintings.

- Notable works: "Jimson Weed" (oil on linen, 1936) depicts large jimson weed blossoms; one version of "Jimson Weed" sold for $44.4 million. The 1936 painting is (per passages) in the Indianapolis Museum of Art.

- Early training and development: Influenced by Arthur Wesley Dow's principles. Created watercolors at the University of Virginia during summers 1912–1914. In 1915 she produced radical charcoal drawings marking a major move toward abstraction; Alfred Stieglitz exhibited some of these drawings at his "291" gallery.

- Residences and sites:

question: Who is  Georgia O'Keeffe?
baseline_hop2_query: Georgia O'Keeffe Alfred Stieglitz relationship biography major works "Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1" "Black Iris" "Radiator Building—Night, New York" "Sky Above Clouds" catalogue raisonné exhibitions collections critical reception
hop2_query: Georgia O'Keeffe Alfred Stieglitz relationship biography major works "Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1" "Black Iris" "Radiator Building—Night, New York" "Sky Above Clouds" catalogue raisonné exhibitions collections critical reception Red and Orange Streak
hop2_docs: Black Iris (painting) | Black Iris, sometimes called Black Iris III, is a 1926 oil painting of by Georgia O'Keeffe.  Art historian Linda Nochlin interpreted Black Iris as a morphological metaphor for female genitalia.  O'Keeffe rejected such interpretations in a 1939 text accompanying an exhibition of her work by writing: "Well—I made you take time to look at what I saw and when you took time to really notice my flower you hung all your own associations with flowers on my flower and you write about my flower as if I think and see what you think and see of the flower—and I don't."  She attempted to do away with sexualized readings of her work by adding a lot of detail.

Flower paintings of Georgia O'Keeffe | Georgia O'Keeffe is best known for her close-up,or large-scale flower paintings, which she painted from the mid-1920s through the 1950s.  She made about 200 paintings of flowers of the more than 2,000 paintings that she made over her career.  One of her paintings, "Jimson Weed", sold for $44.4 million, making it the most expensive painting sold of a female artist's work .

New York skyscrapers (O'Keeffe) | Georgia O'Keeffe created a series of paintings of New York skyscrapers between 1925 and 1929.  They were made after O'Keeffe moved with her new husband into an apartment on the 30th floor of the Shelton Hotel, which gave her expansive views of all but the west side of the city.  She expressed her appreciation of the city's early skyscrapers that were built by the end of the 1920s.  One of her most notable works, which demonstrates her skill at depicting the buildings in the Precisionist style, is the "Radiator Building—Night, New York", of the American Radiator Building.

Jimson Weed (painting) | Jimson Weed is an oil on linen painting by American artist Georgia O'Keeffe from 1936, located in the Indianapolis Museum of Art, which is in Indianapolis, Indiana, United States.  It depicts four large blossoms of jimson weed, or datura.

Red and Orange Streak | Red and Orange Streak is a 1919 painting by Georgia O'Keeffe.  She painted it in New York, after arriving there from Texas.  In a letter to a friend, O'Keeffe wrote: "the whole thing—lit up—first in one place—then in another with flashes of lightning—sometimes just sheet lightning—and sometimes sheet lightning with a sharp bright zigzag flashing across—I … sat on the fence for a long time—just looking at the lightning."

Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 | Charcoal drawings by Georgia O'Keeffe from 1915 represents Georgia O'Keeffe's first major exploration of abstract art and attainment of a freedom to explore her artistic talents based upon what she felt and envisioned.  O'Keeffe developed radical charcoal drawings, with just a few lines, that led to greater development of total abstraction.  This series of works was completed following three summers of instruction at the University of Virginia on Arthur Wesley Dow's design philosophies, which were highly influential in her development as an abstract artist.  Early the following year, photographer and art dealer, Alfred Stieglitz exhibited some of the drawings at his "291" art gallery.

Alfred Stieglitz | Alfred Stieglitz (January 1, 1864 – July 13, 1946) was an American photographer and modern art promoter who was instrumental over his fifty-year career in making photography an accepted art form.  In addition to his photography, Stieglitz was known for the New York art galleries that he ran in the early part of the 20th century, where he introduced many avant-garde European artists to the U.S.  He was married to painter Georgia O'Keeffe.
summary_2:
- Identity and dates:
  - Georgia Totto O'Keeffe (November 15, 1887 – March 6, 1986). American modernist painter, widely called the "Mother of American modernism."

- Primary subjects, style, and influence:
  - Best known for enlarged close-up flower paintings, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes.
  - Worked in modernist and sometimes Precisionist modes; early abstraction developed via charcoal drawings.
  - Influenced by Arthur Wesley Dow's design principles (instruction at the University of Virginia).

- Key works and dates:
  - Black Iris (aka Black Iris III), 1926 — example of her flower paintings; she resisted sexualized readings of her flowers.
  - Jimson Weed, 1936 (oil on linen) — depicts four large jimson weed blossoms; located in the Indianapolis Museum of Art. One of her flower paintings ("Jimson Weed") later sold for $44.4 million, making it the most expensive painting sold of a female artist's work.
  - Series of New York skyscraper paintings, 1925–1929 — includes "Radiator Building—Night, New York" (depicting the American Radiator Building).
  - Charcoal drawings from 1915 mark a major early exploration of abstraction (produced after summers of instruction at the University of Virginia under Dow-influenced teachings).

- Output and productivity:
  - Made over 2,000 paintings in her career; about 200 of those are flower paintings (mid-1920s through the 1950s).

- Residences, museum, and sites (with specifics):
  - Georgia O'Keeffe Home and Studio, 12 Palvadera Drive, Abiquiú, New Mexico — her principal residence and studio from 1945 to 1984; designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998; now a historic house museum with guided tours managed by the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum.
  - Georgia O'Keeffe Museum, 217 Johnson Street, Santa Fe, New Mexico — museum dedicated to her legacy; opened July 17, 1997; focuses on O'Keeffe and American modernism.

- Associates and promotion:
  - Alfred Stieglitz (January 1, 1864 – July 13, 1946) — American photographer and modern art promoter; exhibited some of O'Keeffe's early drawings at his "291" gallery and was married to her (he was instrumental in promoting her work).
  - Other associates noted in context: Maria Chabot (friend and collaborator) and Barbara Buhler Lynes (leading scholar and founding curator/director at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum Research Center).

- Notable interpretive and exhibition facts:
  - O'Keeffe publicly rejected sexualized interpretations of her flower paintings (quoting her 1939 remark that viewers project their associations onto her flowers).
  - Early works include watercolors dated 1912–1914 (University of Virginia period) and charcoal drawings from 1915.

In short: Georgia O'Keeffe was a major American modernist painter (1887–1986), famous for enlarged flower paintings, New York skyscrapers, and New Mexico landscapes; key works include Black Iris (1926) and Jimson Weed (1936); she lived and worked at 12 Palvadera Drive, Abiquiú (1945–1984), and her legacy is preserved at the Georgia O'Keeffe Museum (217 Johnson Street, Santa Fe), opened 1997.

hop2_docs: Social Democratic Party (UK, 1990–present) | The Social Democratic Party is a minor political party in the United Kingdom, established in 1990.  It traces its origin to the Social Democratic Party that was formed in 1981 by a group of dissident Labour Party Members of Parliament (MPs) and former MPs: Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, who became known as the "Gang of Four".  This party merged with the Liberal Party in 1988 to form the Liberal Democrats, but Owen, two other MPs and a minority of party activists formed a breakaway group immediately after with the same name.  That party dissolved itself in 1990, but some activists met and voted to continue the party in defiance of its National Executive, leading to the creation of a new Social Democratic Party.

Social Democratic Party (UK, 1988) | The Social Democratic Party (SDP) formed in 1988 was a political party in the United Kingdom led by David Owen which lasted for only two years.  The party was formed as a result of the original Social Democratic Party, created in 1981 by the "Gang of Four" (Owen, Roy Jenkins, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, all dissident Labour former ministers) voting to turn its electoral alliance with the Liberal Party into a full merger of the two parties.  The new Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD) party thus gained all of the records and assets of the original SDP.
Leader of the Liberal Party (UK) | The Liberal Party was formally established in 1859 and existed until merging with the Social Democratic Party in 1988 to create the Liberal Democrats.

List of Social Democratic Party (UK) MPs | This is a list of Social Democratic Party MPs.  It includes all Members of Parliament elected to the British House of Commons representing the Social Democratic Party.  Members of the Scottish Parliament or the European Parliament are not listed.

Social Democratic Liberal Party | The Social Democratic Liberal Party is a political party in Fiji.  The party was formed in January 2013 after the dissolution of the Soqosoqo Duavata ni Lewenivanua.  The party's leader is Sitiveni Rabuka.

Romanian Social Democratic Party (1990–2001) | The Romanian Social Democratic Party (Romanian: "Partidul Social Democrat Român" , PSDR) was a social-democratic political party in Romania.  Founded in 1990, after the fall of the Communist party rule in Romania, it claimed to be the direct successor of the Romanian Social Democratic Party existing between 1927 and 1948, when it had merged with the Communist Party of Romania to create the Romanian Workers' Party.  Joining the Socialist International, the party entered alliances with the Democratic Party (forming "Uniunea Social-Democrată" during the 1996 legislative election), and took part in the Romanian Democratic Convention governments of Victor Ciorbea, Radu Vasile, and Mugur Isărescu (briefly leading the coalition government with Alexandru Athanasiu in 1999).  The PSDR adhered to the Social Democratic Pole of Romania, and fused into the Social Democratic Party on January 16, 2001.

Social Democratic Party (Angola) | The Social Democratic Party ("Partido Social-Democrata") is a political party in Angola.  The party was founded in Luanda on November 16, 1988.  The party is led by Bengui Pedro João.

hop2_docs: 2012 New Orleans Saints season | The 2012 New Orleans Saints season was the franchise's 46th season in the National Football League, and the 37th with home games at the Superdome.  It "was" going to be the seventh season under head coach Sean Payton; however, he was suspended by the NFL for the entire 2012 season as part of the punishment for the team's bounty scandal.  On April 12, 2012, linebackers coach Joe Vitt was named interim head coach to replace Sean Payton while he served his one-year suspension.  On August 22, 2012, it was announced that Aaron Kromer would take over while Vitt himself served a six-game suspension to start the regular season.  The Saints attempted to make history as the first host team to play the Super Bowl on their own home field, but they were eliminated from post-season contention in Week 16.  The Saints set an NFL record for most yards given up by a defense, 7,042 yards, surpassing the 1981 Baltimore Colts record of 6,793 yards.

2011–12 Elitserien suspensions and fines | The following is a list of all suspensions and fines enforced in the Elitserien during the 2011–12 Elitserien season.  It lists which players or coaches of what team have been punished for which offense and the amount of punishment they have received.  Note that a value of "N/A" in the "Length" or "Fine" column indicates that the player or coach was not suspended or fined for the particular incident.  If a particular suspension lasts longer than a month, no fines are imposed.  Each suspension covers not only Elitserien games but also ice hockey in general – for example, international tournaments such as the Euro Hockey Tour or the European Trophy.  However, this article only mentions the amount of Elitserien games each suspension covers.  Note that the two suspensions on 1 November and the two suspensions on 7 November 2011 are unrelated to each other.

Suspension bridge | A suspension bridge is a type of bridge in which the deck (the load-bearing portion) is hung below suspension cables on vertical suspenders.  The first modern examples of this type of bridge were built in the early 19th century.  Simple suspension bridges, which lack vertical suspenders, have a long history in many mountainous parts of the world.

National Football League player conduct policy | On April 10, 2007, the National Football League introduced a new conduct policy to help control off-field behavior by its players and preserve the league's public image.  The policy, introduced by NFL commissioner Roger Goodell, implements a tougher, new personal-conduct policy, and under conditions of the previous policy handed down two of the harshest suspensions in NFL history for off-field misdeeds.  Each player that has been suspended must reapply for reinstatement.  The policy only applies to the player's personal lives and image in the public spotlight.  The NFL conducts separate investigations for drug and alcohol abuse and performance enhancement.

2014–15 NHL suspensions and fines | The following is a list of all suspensions and fines enforced in the National Hockey League (NHL) during the 2014–15 NHL season.  It lists which players or coaches of what team have been punished for which offense and the amount of punishment they have received.

Suspension of disbelief | The term suspension of disbelief or willing suspension of disbelief has been defined as a willingness to suspend one's critical faculties and believe the unbelievable; sacrifice of realism and logic for the sake of enjoyment.  The term was coined in 1817 by the poet and aesthetic philosopher Samuel Taylor Coleridge, who suggested that if a writer could infuse a "human interest and a semblance of truth" into a fantastic tale, the reader would suspend judgement concerning the implausibility of the narrative.  Suspension of disbelief often applies to fictional works of the action, comedy, fantasy, and horror genres.  Cognitive estrangement in fiction involves using a person's ignorance to promote suspension of disbelief.

Suspension (chemistry) | In chemistry, a suspension is a heterogeneous mixture containing solid particles that are sufficiently large for sedimentation.  Usually they must be larger than one micrometer.  A suspension is a heterogeneous mixture in which the solute particles do not dissolve but get suspended throughout the bulk of the medium.  Particles of suspension are visible to the naked eye.

The new Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD) party thus gained all of the records and assets of the original SDP.

Limehouse Declaration | The Limehouse Declaration was a statement issued on 25 January 1981 by four senior British Labour politicians, all MPs or former MPs and Cabinet Ministers: Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams.  It became known as the Limehouse Declaration as it was made from David Owen's London home in Limehouse.

Robert Dale Owen | Robert Dale Owen (November 7, 1801 – June 24, 1877) was a Scottish-born American social reformer who was a longtime exponent in the United States of the socialist doctrines of his father, Robert Owen, as well as a politician in the Democratic Party.  He served in Congress, where he successfully pushed through the Smithsonian Institution bill and served on the first Board of Regents.

Free Democratic Party (GDR) | The Free Democratic Party (GDR) (German: "Freie Demokratische Partei" , DDR) was an opposition political party in East Germany.  The appeal for its formation was made on 25 November 1989 in Berlin by those East German liberals who doubted the ability of the former block party Liberal Democratic Party of Germany to reform itself.  It was formally founded 4 February 1990, and 12 February 1990 it joined the Association of Free Democrats for the Volkskammer elections.

Owen (Cambridgeshire cricketer) | Owen (first name and dates unknown) was an English first-class cricketer associated with Cambridge Town Club who was active in the 1820s.  He is recorded in one match, totalling 16 runs with a highest score of 8.

It traces its origin to the Social Democratic Party that was formed in 1981 by a group of dissident Labour Party Members of Parliament (MPs) and former MPs: Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, who became known as the "Gang of Four".  This party merged with the Liberal Party in 1988 to form the Liberal Democrats, but Owen, two other MPs and a minority of party activists formed a breakaway group immediately after with the same name.  That party dissolved itself in 1990, but some activists met and voted to continue the party in defiance of its National Executive, leading to the creation of a new Social Democratic Party.

The new Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD) party thus gained all of the records and assets of the original SDP.

SDP–Liberal Alliance | The SDP–Liberal Alliance was a centrist political and electoral alliance in the United Kingdom.  Formed by the Social Democratic Party (SDP) and the Liberal Party, the Alliance existed from 1981 to 1988, when the two parties merged to form the Social and Liberal Democrats, later renamed the Liberal Democrats.

He served as a MP from 1965 to 1997 and as a Member of the Scottish Parliament (MSP) from 1999 to 2003, during which time he was the parliament's Presiding Officer.  Since 1997, he has been a member of the House of Lords.

In 1988, the Liberals merged with the Social Democratic Party, formed by dissident Labour Party members in 1981, to create the Liberal Democrats.

Democracy and Peace Party | The Democracy and Peace Party (Burmese: ဒီမိုကရေစီနှင့် ငြိမ်းချမ်းရေးပါတီ , ] ; abbreviated DPP; formerly the League for Democracy and Peace) is a Burmese political party registered in May 2010, with headquarters in Pabedan Township, Yangon.  It is contesting the 2010 general election in Burma.  DPP's chairman is Aung Than, a high court attorney and a member of the League for Democracy and Peace (ဒီမိုကရေစီနှင့် ငြိမ်းချမ်းရေး အဖွဲ့ချုပ် ), a political party founded by former Prime Minister U Nu in 1988. hop2_docs: Social Democratic Party (UK, 1990–present) | The Social Democratic Party is a minor political party in the United Kingdom, established in 1990.  It traces its origin to the Social Democratic Party that was formed in 1981 by a group of dissident Labour Party Members of Parliament (MPs) and former MPs: Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, who became known as the "Gang of Four".  This party merged with the Liberal Party in 1988 to form the Liberal Democrats, but Owen, two other MPs and a minority of party activists formed a breakaway group immediately after with the same name.  That party dissolved itself in 1990, but some activists met and voted to continue the party in defiance of its National Executive, leading to the creation of a new Social Democratic Party.

List of Social Democratic Party (UK) MPs | This is a list of Social Democratic Party MPs.  It includes all Members of Parliament elected to the British House of Commons representing the Social Democratic Party.  Members of the Scottish Parliament or the European Parliament are not listed.

List of Social Democratic and Labour Party MPs | This is a list of Social Democratic and Labour Party MPs.  It includes all Members of Parliament elected to the British House of Commons representing the Social Democratic and Labour Party.  Members of the European Parliament are not listed.

Social Democratic Party (UK, 1988) | The Social Democratic Party (SDP) formed in 1988 was a political party in the United Kingdom led by David Owen which lasted for only two years.  The party was formed as a result of the original Social Democratic Party, created in 1981 by the "Gang of Four" (Owen, Roy Jenkins, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, all dissident Labour former ministers) voting to turn its electoral alliance with the Liberal Party into a full merger of the two parties.  The new Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD) party thus gained all of the records and assets of the original SDP.

Republican and Socialist Front | Republican and Socialist Front (Portuguese: "Frente Republicana e Socialista" , or FRS) was an electoral alliance in Portugal, consisting of the Socialist Party (PS), Leftwing Union for the Socialist Democracy (UEDS) and Independent Social Democratic Action (ASDI).  FRS contested the 1980 elections.  It got 1,606,198 votes (26.6%) and 71 MPs elected (PS 66 MPs, UEDS and ASDI 4 each).  It dissolved in 1982 when PS decided to join coalitions with PSD.

List of Democratic Unionist Party MPs | This is a list of Democratic Unionist Party MPs.  It includes all Members of Parliament elected to the British House of Commons representing the Democratic Unionist Party.  Members of the European Parliament or the Northern Ireland Assembly are not listed.

Portuguese legislative election, 1995 | The Portuguese legislative election of 1995 took place on 1 October.  The Socialist Party defeated the Social Democratic Party under the lead of António Guterres, elected three years before, but missed the absolute majority by 4 MPs.  The Social Democratic party under the lead of Fernando Nogueira was weakened by the end of a ten years cycle of government of Cavaco Silva.  The two minor parties, the People's Party and the Democratic Unity Coalition achieved only 15 MPs each, a thing that marked the growing bi-polarization of the Portuguese political map.  Although turnout, in percentage point, was lower than the previous election in 1991, almost 6 million voters cast a ballot on election day, the highest figure since 1980.

question: The Social Democratic Party (SDP) formed in 1988 was a political party in the United Kingdom led by David Owen which lasted for?

hop2_docs: Social Democratic Party (UK, 1990–present) | The Social Democratic Party is a minor political party in the United Kingdom, established in 1990.  It traces its origin to the Social Democratic Party that was formed in 1981 by a group of dissident Labour Party Members of Parliament (MPs) and former MPs: Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, who became known as the "Gang of Four".  This party merged with the Liberal Party in 1988 to form the Liberal Democrats, but Owen, two other MPs and a minority of party activists formed a breakaway group immediately after with the same name.  That party dissolved itself in 1990, but some activists met and voted to continue the party in defiance of its National Executive, leading to the creation of a new Social Democratic Party.

Social Democratic Party (UK, 1988) | The Social Democratic Party (SDP) formed in 1988 was a political party in the United Kingdom led by David Owen which lasted for only two years.  The party was formed as a result of the original Social Democratic Party, created in 1981 by the "Gang of Four" (Owen, Roy Jenkins, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams, all dissident Labour former ministers) voting to turn its electoral alliance with the Liberal Party into a full merger of the two parties.  The new Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD) party thus gained all of the records and assets of the original SDP.

Limehouse Declaration | The Limehouse Declaration was a statement issued on 25 January 1981 by four senior British Labour politicians, all MPs or former MPs and Cabinet Ministers: Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers and Shirley Williams.  It became known as the Limehouse Declaration as it was made from David Owen's London home in Limehouse.

William D. Owen | William Dale Owen (his middle name is given as "Dunn" in some references) (September 6, 1846 – date of death unknown) was a U.S. Representative from Indiana.  Before serving in Congress he was a clergyman, attorney, newspaper editor, and the author of two books.  After serving in Congress and as Secretary of State of Indiana, he engaged in various business ventures, including promotion of coffee and rubber plantations in Mexico.  In 1905 his business partner was arrested; in 1906 the partner was convicted of fraud and theft, and imprisoned.  Owen left the United States to avoid prosecution; what happened to him after he fled the country is not known.

Robert Dale Owen | Robert Dale Owen (November 7, 1801 – June 24, 1877) was a Scottish-born American social reformer who was a longtime exponent in the United States of the socialist doctrines of his father, Robert Owen, as well as a politician in the Democratic Party.  He served in Congress, where he successfully pushed through the Smithsonian Institution bill and served on the first Board of Regents.

Free Democratic Party (GDR) | The Free Democratic Party (GDR) (German: "Freie Demokratische Partei" , DDR) was an opposition political party in East Germany.  The appeal for its formation was made on 25 November 1989 in Berlin by those East German liberals who doubted the ability of the former block party Liberal Democratic Party of Germany to reform itself.  It was formally founded 4 February 1990, and 12 February 1990 it joined the Association of Free Democrats for the Volkskammer elections.
William David Owen | William David Owen (1874–1925), born William Owen was a Welsh author best known for his novel "Madam Wen" (White Lady).
summary_2:
- Entity: Social Democratic Party (SDP) formed in 1988 (United Kingdom).
- Leader: David Owen.
- Lifespan/duration: Lasted only two years — roughly 1988 to 1990 (passage [2] states "only two years"; passage [1] records the party "dissolved itself in 1990").
- Origin/context: Formed immediately after the original SDP (founded 1981 by the "Gang of Four": Roy Jenkins, David Owen, Bill Rodgers, Shirley Williams) merged with the Liberal Party in 1988 to create the Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD); the SLD gained the original SDP’s records and assets.
- Composition: Owen, two other MPs and a minority of activists formed the breakaway 1988 SDP.
- Aftermath: The 1988 SDP dissolved in 1990; some activists nonetheless continued the name, leading to a new Social Democratic Party established in 1990 (the minor party extant from 1990–present).
- Geography/identifiers: United Kingdom; related parties include the original SDP (1981), the Liberal Party, and the Social and Liberal Democrats (SLD, later the Liberal Democrats).

hop2_docs: The Beast (roller coaster) | The Beast is a wooden roller coaster located at Kings Island in Mason, Ohio.  Built in-house, it opened in 1979 as the tallest, fastest, and longest wooden roller coaster in the world.  The Beast remains the longest wooden coaster at 7359 ft and spans more than 35 acre , utilizing the surrounding terrain for many of its elements.  Two lift hills contribute to the ride's duration of more than four minutes, which also ranks as one of the longest among roller coasters.

Hurricane: Category 5 | Hurricane: Category 5 was a Custom Coasters International wooden roller coaster located at the Myrtle Beach Pavilion.  It replaced the Corkscrew roller coaster which existed since the late 1970s.  The Pavilion unveiled their multimillion-dollar coaster May 6, 2000.  During operation, Hurricane held the record for being the tallest, fastest, and longest wooden roller coaster in South Carolina.  The ride closed with the Pavilion on September 30, 2006.  Although Burroughs & Chapin attempted to sell the ride along with the Haunted Hotel, Log Flume, Treasure Hunt, and a few other rides, the ride was deemed too expensive a task to dismantle and relocate, and was ultimately demolished in March 2007.  The only part of the ride not demolished were the two Gerstlauer trains used on the ride.  These trains were shipped to Kings Island, an amusement park in Mason, Ohio.  They were then repainted and installed on Son of Beast, which was at the time the world's tallest and fastest wooden roller coaster.  Son of Beast was later demolished on November 20, 2012, following an incident that occurred in 2009.

Excalibur (Valleyfair) | Excalibur is a steel roller coaster with a wooden structure located at Valleyfair in Shakopee, Minnesota.  It was built in 1989 by Arrow Dynamics, for the cost of $3,000,000.  The ride is 105 feet (32 m) tall with a top speed of 54.5 mph (87.7 km/h), and has a minimum height requirement of 48 inches.  Excalibur is only one of a few coasters in the world that has a steel track with a wood frame.  Two others, Gemini and Cedar Creek Mine Ride, are located at sister park Cedar Point in Sandusky, Ohio.  It follows a customized figure-eight track layout and is rather short for a major roller coaster, with a total ride duration of two minutes and thirteen seconds.  Most likely due to its location at the very western edge of the park and the more recently built roller coasters such as Wild Thing, Steel Venom and Renegade.

Originally named "Wilde Beast", it is one of the four roller coasters that debuted with the park in 1981, and is one of two wooden coasters at Canada's Wonderland modelled after a ride at Coney Island amusement park in Cincinnati, Ohio (specifically, Wildcat); the other is the Mighty Canadian Minebuster.  The ride's fan curve was rebuilt in 1998.

Flying roller coaster | A flying roller coaster is a type of roller coaster meant to simulate the sensations of flight by harnessing riders in a prone position during the duration of the ride.  The roller coaster cars are suspended below the track, with riders secured such that their backs are parallel to the track.  Flying roller coasters come in a variety of sizes and designs depending on the intended demographic for the ride.  Some flying roller coasters are intended for children and, thus, are relatively slow and gentle; others are meant for older children and adults and can be very fast and intense.

The Bush Beast | The Bush Beast was a wooden roller coaster located at Wonderland Sydney in Australia.  The ride opened with the park on 7 December 1985 and closed with the park on 26 April 2004.  The ride features a mirrored layout to that of other wooden roller coasters located at California's Great America, Canada's Wonderland, and Kings Dominion.  "The Bush Beast" was manufactured by Taft Broadcasting.

Mystic Timbers | Mystic Timbers is a wooden roller coaster located at Kings Island amusement park in Mason, Ohio.  Constructed by Great Coasters International, the roller coaster opened in the park's Rivertown section on April 15, 2017.  Mystic Timbers is the park's fourth wooden coaster, and its opening allowed Kings Island to reclaim the title of having the longest collection of wooden coaster track in the world with 18804 ft . Hamlet on screen | Over fifty films of William Shakespeare's "Hamlet" have been made since 1900. Known as "America's Roller Coast", the park features a world-record 71 rides, including 16 roller coasters – the second-most in the world behind Six Flags Magic Mountain. question: What movie was directed by an Italian director, former senator and distant relative of Leonardo da Vinci?
baseline_hop2_query: Franco Zeffirelli filmography list of films directed (feature films) with release years
hop2_query: Franco Zeffirelli filmography list of films directed (feature films) with release years
hop2_docs: Florence: Days of Destruction | Florence: Days of Destruction (Italian: Per Firenze) is a 1966 documentary about the 1966 Flood of the Arno River and its catastrophic effect on the city of Florence.  Directed by Franco Zeffirelli, it is Zeffirelli's only documentary, and features the only known film footage of the flood.  The film is 50 minutes long, and was produced by RAI.  Released less than a month after the disaster, the film reputedly raised more than $20 million for the reconstruction efforts.

Cavalleria rusticana (1982 film) | Cavalleria rusticana is a 1982 Italian film directed by Franco Zeffirelli based on Pietro Mascagni's 1890 opera of the same name.  It stars tenor Plácido Domingo, mezzo-soprano Elena Obraztsova, and baritone Renato Bruson, all singing their own roles.  Georges Prêtre conducted the Teatro alla Scala Orchestra for the movie's soundtrack.  The film was made for broadcast on television.  In 2003, it was released on DVD by Deutsche Grammophon, paired with "Pagliacci", also starring Plácido Domingo and directed by Franco Zeffirelli.

David Lean filmography | The following is a filmography of David Lean, whose body of work in the film industry spanned the period from 1930–1984.  This list includes the release year of the film, the role(s) Lean had in the production of each film, and additional notes such as awards and nominations.  Lean directed 17 feature films in total.

The Champ (1979 film) | The Champ is a 1979 American drama sports film directed by Franco Zeffirelli and a remake of the 1931 Academy Award-winning film of the same name which was directed by King Vidor.  It stars Jon Voight, Faye Dunaway, and Ricky Schroder.  It is also the final film for actress Joan Blondell to be released during her lifetime.  Blondell, who died from leukemia on Christmas Day eight months later, also starred in two other films that were released after her death.

Hamlet on screen | Over fifty films of William Shakespeare's "Hamlet" have been made since 1900.  Seven post-war "Hamlet" films have had a theatrical release: Laurence Olivier's "Hamlet" of 1948; Grigori Kozintsev's 1964 Russian adaptation; a film of the John Gielgud-directed 1964 Broadway production, "Richard Burton's Hamlet", which played limited engagements that same year; Tony Richardson's 1969 version (the first in colour) featuring Nicol Williamson as Hamlet and Anthony Hopkins as Claudius; Franco Zeffirelli's 1990 version starring Mel Gibson; Kenneth Branagh's full-text 1996 version; and Michael Almereyda's 2000 modernisation, starring Ethan Hawke.

Callas Forever | Callas Forever is a 2002 biographical film directed by Franco Zeffirelli, who co-wrote the screenplay with Martin Sherman.  It is an homage to Zeffirelli's friend, internationally acclaimed opera diva Maria Callas, whom he directed on stage in "Norma", "La Traviata", and "Tosca".

Hamlet (1990 film) | Hamlet is a 1990 drama film based on the Shakespearean tragedy of the same name, directed by Franco Zeffirelli and starring Mel Gibson as the eponymous character.  The film also features Glenn Close, Alan Bates, Paul Scofield, Ian Holm, Helena Bonham Carter, Stephen Dillane, and Nathaniel Parker.  An international co-production between the United States, the United Kingdom, and France, the film is notable for being the first film produced by Icon Productions, a company co-founded by Gibson.
summary_2:
- Director: Franco Zeffirelli (born 12 February 1923). He is an Italian director/producer of opera, films and television, served as a senator for Forza Italia (1994–2001), and has been identified by researchers as a distant relative of Leonardo da Vinci (passage [1] / context).

- Films/works directed by Franco Zeffirelli mentioned in the passages:

  - The Champ — 1979 American drama film directed by Zeffirelli; stars Jon Voight, Faye Dunaway, and Ricky Schroder (passage [4]).
  - Cavalleria rusticana — 1982 Italian film directed by Zeffirelli, based on Mascagni's opera; stars Plácido Domingo, Elena Obraztsova, Renato Bruson; made for broadcast on television (passage [2]).
  - Hamlet — 1990 drama film directed by Zeffirelli; stars Mel Gibson (passages [5], [7]).
  - Callas Forever — 2002 biographical film directed (and co-written) by Zeffirelli (passage [6]).
  - (Context notes) La Vita di Leonardo Da Vinci / The Life of Leonardo Da Vinci — 1971 Italian television miniseries dramatizing Leonardo's life (context).

- Direct answer (examples that match the description): Any of the above were directed by the Italian director/former senator/distant relative Franco Zeffirelli — e.g., Hamlet (1990), The Champ (1979), Cavalleria rusticana (1982), Callas Forever (2002), or the 1966 documentary Florence: Days of Destruction.

The ride was manufactured by Swiss firm Bolliger & Mabillard.  Green Lantern is fairly similar to the former Mantis, stand-up coaster at Cedar Point, but is taller, faster, and features one more inversion.

Excalibur (Valleyfair) | Excalibur is a steel roller coaster with a wooden structure located at Valleyfair in Shakopee, Minnesota.  It was built in 1989 by Arrow Dynamics, for the cost of $3,000,000.  The ride is 105 feet (32 m) tall with a top speed of 54.5 mph (87.7 km/h), and has a minimum height requirement of 48 inches.  Excalibur is only one of a few coasters in the world that has a steel track with a wood frame.  Two others, Gemini and Cedar Creek Mine Ride, are located at sister park Cedar Point in Sandusky, Ohio.  It follows a customized figure-eight track layout and is rather short for a major roller coaster, with a total ride duration of two minutes and thirteen seconds.  Most likely due to its location at the very western edge of the park and the more recently built roller coasters such as Wild Thing, Steel Venom and Renegade.

VertiGo (ride) | VertiGo was a thrill ride located at Cedar Point and Knott's Berry Farm.  Both the rides opened in 2001 and both were designed by S&S Worldwide.  After an incident at Cedar Point, both rides were demolished for the 2002 season.

Cedar Creek Mine Ride | Cedar Creek Mine Ride is a mine train roller coaster at Cedar Point amusement park in Sandusky, Ohio.  Built by Arrow Dynamics, the roller coaster opened in 1969 in the Frontiertown section of the park.  It is the second oldest roller coaster at Cedar Point behind Blue Streak.  Cedar Creek Mine Ride is one of two roller coasters at Cedar Point to utilize two separate lift hills – the other is Iron Dragon.  s of 2006 , it has accommodated over 62 million riders.

Zoomerang (Lake Compounce) | Zoomerang is the name of a boomerang steel roller coaster located at Lake Compounce in Bristol, Connecticut.  Zoomerang is one of the forty-five functioning boomerang style roller coasters in the world.  They all feature a cobra roll and a loop, but the Lake Compounce Boomerang was the first boomerang to receive a Vekoma designed train.  Early models used trains designed by Arrow Dynamics.  In September 2007, due to paint deterioration, the ride was repainted with a new color scheme with purple tracks and teal supports.

It stars tenor Plácido Domingo, mezzo-soprano Elena Obraztsova, and baritone Renato Bruson, all singing their own roles.  Georges Prêtre conducted the Teatro alla Scala Orchestra for the movie's soundtrack.  The film was made for broadcast on television.  In 2003, it was released on DVD by Deutsche Grammophon, paired with "Pagliacci", also starring Plácido Domingo and directed by Franco Zeffirelli.

Callas Forever | Callas Forever is a 2002 biographical film directed by Franco Zeffirelli, who co-wrote the screenplay with Martin Sherman.  It is an homage to Zeffirelli's friend, internationally acclaimed opera diva Maria Callas, whom he directed on stage in "Norma", "La Traviata", and "Tosca".

Florence: Days of Destruction | Florence: Days of Destruction (Italian: Per Firenze) is a 1966 documentary about the 1966 Flood of the Arno River and its catastrophic effect on the city of Florence.  Directed by Franco Zeffirelli, it is Zeffirelli's only documentary, and features the only known film footage of the flood.  The film is 50 minutes long, and was produced by RAI.  Released less than a month after the disaster, the film reputedly raised more than $20 million for the reconstruction efforts.

Young Toscanini | Young Toscanini (original title: Il giovane Toscanini) is a 1988 Italian-French biographical drama film directed by Franco Zeffirelli and starring C. Thomas Howell and Elizabeth Taylor.

Franco Zeffirelli | Franco Zeffirelli, KBE Grande Ufficiale OMRI (] ; born 12 February 1923) is an Italian director and producer of operas, films and television.  He is also a former senator (1994–2001) for the Italian centre-right "Forza Italia" party.  Recently, Italian researchers have found that he is one of the few distant relatives of Leonardo da Vinci.

Sparrow (1993 film) | Sparrow (Italian: "Storia di una capinera" ) is a 1993 Italian drama film directed by Franco Zeffirelli.  It is an adaptation of Giovanni Verga's novel, "Storia di una capinera" and was filmed in Sicily in 1993.  It stars Angela Bettis, and premiered at the Tokyo International Film Festival in October 1993.

Endless Love (1981 film) | Endless Love is a 1981 American romantic drama film based on Scott Spencer's 1979 novel of the same name.  The film is directed by Franco Zeffirelli, and stars Brooke Shields and Martin Hewitt.  The screenplay was written by Judith Rascoe.  The original music score was composed by Jonathan Tunick.

hop2_docs: The Beast (roller coaster) | The Beast is a wooden roller coaster located at Kings Island in Mason, Ohio.  Built in-house, it opened in 1979 as the tallest, fastest, and longest wooden roller coaster in the world.  The Beast remains the longest wooden coaster at 7359 ft and spans more than 35 acre , utilizing the surrounding terrain for many of its elements.  Two lift hills contribute to the ride's duration of more than four minutes, which also ranks as one of the longest among roller coasters.

Cedar Point | Cedar Point is a 364 acre amusement park located on a Lake Erie peninsula in Sandusky, Ohio.  Opened in 1870, it is the second-oldest operating amusement park in the United States behind Lake Compounce.  Cedar Point is owned and operated by Cedar Fair and is considered the flagship of the amusement park chain.  Known as "America's Roller Coast", the park features a world-record 71 rides, including 16 roller coasters – the second-most in the world behind Six Flags Magic Mountain.  Its newest roller coaster, Valravn, opened in May 2016.

Excalibur (Valleyfair) | Excalibur is a steel roller coaster with a wooden structure located at Valleyfair in Shakopee, Minnesota.  It was built in 1989 by Arrow Dynamics, for the cost of $3,000,000.  The ride is 105 feet (32 m) tall with a top speed of 54.5 mph (87.7 km/h), and has a minimum height requirement of 48 inches.  Excalibur is only one of a few coasters in the world that has a steel track with a wood frame.  Two others, Gemini and Cedar Creek Mine Ride, are located at sister park Cedar Point in Sandusky, Ohio.  It follows a customized figure-eight track layout and is rather short for a major roller coaster, with a total ride duration of two minutes and thirteen seconds.  Most likely due to its location at the very western edge of the park and the more recently built roller coasters such as Wild Thing, Steel Venom and Renegade.

Son of Beast | Son of Beast was a record-breaking wooden roller coaster located at Kings Island in Mason, Ohio.  Built and designed by the now defunct Roller Coaster Corporation of America, it opened to the public on May 26, 2000, and was themed as a sequel to one of the park's other signature attractions, The Beast.  In addition to breaking the world record for speed, Son of Beast was the first wooden hypercoaster in the world with a 214 ft drop and was the second wooden coaster to feature an inversion; Flip Flap Railway was the first, but was long defunct before the ride's opening.

Green Lantern (Six Flags Great Adventure) | Green Lantern is a steel stand-up roller coaster located at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson Township, New Jersey.  "Green Lantern" stands 155 ft tall and features a top speed of 63 mph .  The 4155 ft ride features five inversions and a duration of approximately 2⁄ minutes.  The ride was manufactured by Swiss firm Bolliger & Mabillard.  Green Lantern is fairly similar to the former Mantis, stand-up coaster at Cedar Point, but is taller, faster, and features one more inversion.

Millennium Force | Millennium Force is a steel roller coaster built by Intamin at Cedar Point amusement park in Sandusky, Ohio, United States.  It was the fourteenth roller coaster to be built at the park since Blue Streak opened in 1964.  Upon completion in 2000, Millennium Force broke six world records and was the world's first Giga Coaster, a roller coaster that exceeds 300 ft in height and completes a full circuit.  It was briefly the tallest and fastest in the world until Steel Dragon 2000 opened later the same year.  The ride is also the third-longest roller coaster in North America after The Beast at Kings Island and Fury 325 at Carowinds.  It was the first roller coaster to use a cable lift system rather than a traditional chain lift.  The coaster has a 310 ft , 45-degree lift hill with a 300 ft drop and features two tunnels, three overbanked turns, and four hills.  It has a top speed of 93 mph .

Hurricane: Category 5 | Hurricane: Category 5 was a Custom Coasters International wooden roller coaster located at the Myrtle Beach Pavilion.  It replaced the Corkscrew roller coaster which existed since the late 1970s.  The Pavilion unveiled their multimillion-dollar coaster May 6, 2000.  During operation, Hurricane held the record for being the tallest, fastest, and longest wooden roller coaster in South Carolina.  The ride closed with the Pavilion on September 30, 2006.  Although Burroughs & Chapin attempted to sell the ride along with the Haunted Hotel, Log Flume, Treasure Hunt, and a few other rides, the ride was deemed too expensive a task to dismantle and relocate, and was ultimately demolished in March 2007.  The only part of the ride not demolished were the two Gerstlauer trains used on the ride.  These trains were shipped to Kings Island, an amusement park in Mason, Ohio.  They were then repainted and installed on Son of Beast, which was at the time the world's tallest and fastest wooden roller coaster.  Son of Beast was later demolished on November 20, 2012, following an incident that occurred in 2009.

The film was directed by Franco Zeffirelli and stars Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton as Shakespeare's Kate and Petruchio.

The Taming of the Shrew on screen | There have been numerous on screen adaptations of Shakespeare's "The Taming of the Shrew".  The best known cinematic adaptations are Sam Taylor's 1929 "The Taming of the Shrew" and Franco Zeffirelli's 1967 "The Taming of the Shrew", both of which starred the most famous celebrity couples of their era; Mary Pickford and Douglas Fairbanks in 1929 and Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton in 1967.  On television, perhaps the most significant adaptation is the 1980 "BBC Television Shakespeare" version, directed by Jonathan Miller and starring John Cleese and Sarah Badel.

Romeo and Juliet on screen | William Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet may be one of the most-screened plays of all time.  The most notable theatrical releases were George Cukor's multi-Oscar-nominated 1936 production "Romeo and Juliet", Franco Zeffirelli's 1968 film "Romeo and Juliet", and Baz Luhrmann's 1996 MTV-inspired "Romeo + Juliet".  The latter two were both, at the time, the highest-grossing Shakespeare films.  Cukor featured the mature actors Norma Shearer and Leslie Howard as the teenage lovers while Zeffirelli populated his film with beautiful young people, and Baz Luhrmann produced a heavily cut fast-paced version aimed at teenage audiences.

Callas Forever | Callas Forever is a 2002 biographical film directed by Franco Zeffirelli, who co-wrote the screenplay with Martin Sherman.  It is an homage to Zeffirelli's friend, internationally acclaimed opera diva Maria Callas, whom he directed on stage in "Norma", "La Traviata", and "Tosca".

Romeo & Juliet (2013 film) | Romeo & Juliet is a 2013 internationally co-produced romantic drama film adaptation of William Shakespeare's romantic tragedy of the same name written by Julian Fellowes and directed by Carlo Carlei.  The film stars Douglas Booth, Hailee Steinfeld, Damian Lewis, Kodi Smit-McPhee, Ed Westwick, Stellan Skarsgård and Paul Giamatti.  The film opened in the United Kingdom and the United States on 11 October 2013.  Like Franco Zeffirelli's adaptation of Shakespeare's tragedy, this film uses the traditional setting of Renaissance Verona, but, unlike previous major film adaptations, only follows the plot and uses only some of the dialogue as written by Shakespeare.  This has led to a controversy; several critics denounced the film's advertising as misleading and losing the essence of the play.  The film grossed $3 million.

Hamlet on screen | Over fifty films of William Shakespeare's "Hamlet" have been made since 1900.  Seven post-war "Hamlet" films have had a theatrical release: Laurence Olivier's "Hamlet" of 1948; Grigori Kozintsev's 1964 Russian adaptation; a film of the John Gielgud-directed 1964 Broadway production, "Richard Burton's Hamlet", which played limited engagements that same year; Tony Richardson's 1969 version (the first in colour) featuring Nicol Williamson as Hamlet and Anthony Hopkins as Claudius; Franco Zeffirelli's 1990 version starring Mel Gibson; Kenneth Branagh's full-text 1996 version; and Michael Almereyda's 2000 modernisation, starring Ethan Hawke.

Young Toscanini | Young Toscanini (original title: Il giovane Toscanini) is a 1988 Italian-French biographical drama film directed by Franco Zeffirelli and starring C. Thomas Howell and Elizabeth Taylor.

question: The wooden rollercoaster that supercedes the Millennium Force in duratation is located in which amusement park?
baseline_hop2_query: Boulder Dash ride duration Lake Compounce vs Millennium Force ride duration Cedar Point comparison
hop2_query: Boulder Dash ride duration Lake Compounce vs Millennium Force ride duration Cedar Point comparison Millennium Force The Beast (roller coaster)
hop2_docs: Millennium Force | Millennium Force is a steel roller coaster built by Intamin at Cedar Point amusement park in Sandusky, Ohio, United States.  It was the fourteenth roller coaster to be built at the park since Blue Streak opened in 1964.  Upon completion in 2000, Millennium Force broke six world records and was the world's first Giga Coaster, a roller coaster that exceeds 300 ft in height and completes a full circuit.  It was briefly the tallest and fastest in the world until Steel Dragon 2000 opened later the same year.  The ride is also the third-longest roller coaster in North America after The Beast at Kings Island and Fury 325 at Carowinds.  It was the first roller coaster to use a cable lift system rather than a traditional chain lift.  The coaster has a 310 ft , 45-degree lift hill with a 300 ft drop and features two tunnels, three overbanked turns, and four hills.  It has a top speed of 93 mph .

Intimidator 305 | Intimidator 305 is a steel roller coaster located at Kings Dominion in Doswell, Virginia, United States.  Manufactured by Intamin, Intimidator 305 opened to the public on April 2, 2010, as the park's fourteenth roller coaster.  The roller coaster is located in the Safari Village section of the park near Anaconda on the former site of the Safari Monorail ride.  Standing at 305 ft tall and reaching speeds of 90 mph , it is the second Giga Coaster to be built in North America – the first was Millennium Force at Cedar Point.  The $25 million investment was the largest of any ride in the park's history.  It is named and themed after NASCAR driver Dale Earnhardt, who was also known as the "Intimidator."

Cedar Point | Cedar Point is a 364 acre amusement park located on a Lake Erie peninsula in Sandusky, Ohio.  Opened in 1870, it is the second-oldest operating amusement park in the United States behind Lake Compounce.  Cedar Point is owned and operated by Cedar Fair and is considered the flagship of the amusement park chain.  Known as "America's Roller Coast", the park features a world-record 71 rides, including 16 roller coasters – the second-most in the world behind Six Flags Magic Mountain.  Its newest roller coaster, Valravn, opened in May 2016.

Steel Dragon 2000 | Steel Dragon 2000 (スチールドラゴン2000 , Suchiiru Doragon Nisen ) is a roller coaster at Nagashima Spa Land amusement park in Mie Prefecture, Japan.  Built by Morgan Manufacturing, this coaster opened, appropriately, in 2000—"The Year of the Dragon" in Asia.  It debuted 3 months after Millennium Force at Cedar Point, and surpassed that as the world's tallest complete-circuit coaster.  It lost these records in height in 2003 when Top Thrill Dragster opened at Cedar Point.  It also took the record for the longest track length—2479 m —which it currently holds.  The ride is the second fastest coaster in Japan and the sixth fastest in the world.

Boulder Dash (roller coaster) | Boulder Dash is a wooden roller coaster located at Lake Compounce in Bristol, Connecticut.  Construction for the coaster began in June 1999 and was completed in May 2000.  The coaster was built by Custom Coasters International using Southern Yellow Pine wood; while the track is made of Douglas Fir.

Maverick (roller coaster) | Maverick is a steel roller coaster built by Intamin at Cedar Point in Sandusky, Ohio, United States.  It was the seventeenth roller coaster built at the park since Blue Streak in 1964.  It is notable as being the 500th roller coaster designed by German engineer Werner Stengel and the first roller coaster featuring a Twisted Horseshoe Roll.  Maverick's US$21-million price tag makes it Cedar Point's fourth most expensive roller coaster, behind Millennium Force, Top Thrill Dragster, and GateKeeper.  The 4450 ft course makes it the fourth longest roller coaster at Cedar Point.

The Beast (roller coaster) | The Beast is a wooden roller coaster located at Kings Island in Mason, Ohio.  Built in-house, it opened in 1979 as the tallest, fastest, and longest wooden roller coaster in the world.  The Beast remains the longest wooden coaster at 7359 ft and spans more than 35 acre , utilizing the surrounding terrain for many of its elements.  Two lift hills contribute to the ride's duration of more than four minutes, which also ranks as one of the longest among roller coasters.
